citations.narrative:
Several enclosures at the facility are not adequately protecting the animals from injury or being kept in good repair.
One brown capuchin (Zander) died after gaining access to an electric junction box that was inside the enclosure on 04-
April-2026. During daily observations of the animals, the licensee found the primate had gained access to the unused box
that contained live electrical wires overnight. The animal was still holding onto the exposed electrical wire when it was
found. The electricity was turned off, and the animal was confirmed to be deceased due to electrocution. The licensee
determined the capuchin was able to gain access by breaking/manipulating the plastic cover plate that was utilized to
prevent the animals from contacting the live electrical wiring. The enclosure houses two other brown capuchins and the
junction box had since been covered by a wooden barrier.
One brown capuchin (Gunner) had a ceiling fan that was operating in his indoor enclosure. The enclosure wire walls and
an additional tall metal enclosure contained within the primary enclosure allowed the capuchin to climb and be in close
proximity to the moving fan blades and could result in injury to the animal. The fan had several broken blades and was
oscillating side to side exposing a blue electrical junction box that powered the fan. Additionally, there are several areas
with excessive sharp points present in the fine mesh wire in various areas near the entrance and top third of the
enclosure.
The outdoor area of an enclosure housing two brown capuchins had areas of disrepair. A large area of the wire mesh is
pulling away from the primary enclosure creating many sharp points. Additionally, this exposed area could lead to
entrapment of the primates.
Failure to adequately ensure that enclosures are kept in good repair can lead to escape, injury, or death in the primates.
The facility must ensure that housing facilities for nonhuman primates must be designed and constructed so that they are
structurally sound for the species of nonhuman primates housed in them. They must be kept in good repair, and they
must protect the animals from injury, contain the animals securely, and restrict other animals from entering. Housing
facilities for nonhuman primates must be designed and constructed so that they are structurally sound for the species of
nonhuman primates housed in them. They must be kept in good repair, and they must protect the animals from injury,
contain the animals securely, and restrict other animals from entering. Correct by, 29-April-2026.

The facility is not maintaining the surfaces of housing facilities to be free of excessive rust. The indoor portion of a primary
enclosure housing 1 rhesus macaque and 1 java macaque was observed with excessive rust on the wire mesh paneling
of the wall of the enclosure. Additionally, the lower horizontal support beam in this enclosure has excessive rust with
pitting of the affected area. The non-human primates can come into contact with the rusted surfaces.
Rusted primary enclosure surfaces can lose structural integrity over time and cannot be properly cleaned and sanitized.
The licensee must ensure that any surfaces that come into contact with non-human primates are free of excessive rust
that prevents the required cleaning and sanitization, or that affects the structural strength of the surfaces. Correct by 29-
April-2026.

The facility is not adequately maintaining surfaces in housing facilities on a regular basis. The indoor portion of two
primary enclosures housing a total of 3 rhesus macaques and a java macaque have multiple areas of peeling paint that
exposes the concrete underneath the paint rendering these areas difficult to effectively clean and sanitize. Peeling paint
can also be hazardous to the non-human primates if ingested. Additionally, there is an approximately 18 inch x 9 inch
area of the ceiling in the primary enclosure housing the rhesus macaque and java macaque that is damaged exposing the
raw wood underneath and creating jagged edges that could result in injury to the animals.
Surfaces in non-human primate housing facilities require regular maintenance to keep them in good repair, to allow them
to be readily cleaned and sanitized and to protect the animals from injury. All surfaces of non-human primate housing
facilities must be maintained on a regular basis. Correct by 29-April-2026.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The facility is not adequately removing excreta and food waste daily from primary enclosures. The indoor portion of two
primary enclosures housing a total of 4 brown capuchins and 1 squirrel monkey respectively was observed with excessive
food waste and excreta on the floor of the enclosures.
Excreta and food waste left in enclosures can lead to increased disease hazards and attract pests that can be harmful to
the animals. The facility must ensure all excreta and food waste is removed from inside each primary enclosure daily.
Correct by 29-April-2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
Mark Sanderbeck, ANIMAL CARE INSPECTOR n

narrative:
3.75(a)  Critical
   
Housing facilities, general. 
Several enclosures at the facility are not adequately protecting the animals from injury or being kept in good repair.  
 
One brown capuchin (Zander) died after gaining access to an electric junction box that was inside the enclosure on 04-
April-2026. During daily observations of the animals, the licensee found the primate had gained access to the unused box

determined the capuchin was able to gain access by breaking/manipulating the plastic cover plate that was utilized to 
prevent the animals from contacting the live electrical wiring. The enclosure houses two other brown capuchins and the 
junction box had since been covered by a wooden barrier.  
 
One brown capuchin (Gunner) had a ceiling fan that was operating in his indoor enclosure. The enclosure wire walls and

The outdoor area of an enclosure housing two brown capuchins had areas of disrepair. A large area of the wire mesh is 
pulling away from the primary enclosure creating many sharp points. Additionally, this exposed area could lead to 
entrapment of the primates.  
 
Failure to adequately ensure that enclosures are kept in good repair can lead to escape, injury, or death in the primates.  
 
The facility must ensure that housing facilities for nonhuman primates must be designed and constructed so that they are 
structurally sound for the species of nonhuman primates housed in them. They must be kept in good repair, and they 
must protect the animals from injury, contain the animals securely, and restrict other animals from entering. Housing

nonhuman primates housed in them. They must be kept in good repair, and they must protect the animals from injury, 
contain the animals securely, and restrict other animals from entering. Correct by, 29-April-2026.

3.75(c)(1)(i) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
The facility is not maintaining the surfaces of housing facilities to be free of excessive rust. The indoor portion of a primary 
enclosure housing 1 rhesus macaque and 1 java macaque was observed with excessive rust on the wire mesh paneling 
of the wall of the enclosure. Additionally, the lower horizontal support beam in this enclosure has excessive rust with 
pitting of the affected area. The non-human primates can come into contact with the rusted surfaces.  
 
Rusted primary enclosure surfaces can lose structural integrity over time and cannot be properly cleaned and sanitized. 
The licensee must ensure that any surfaces that come into contact with non-human primates are free of excessive rust 
that prevents the required cleaning and sanitization, or that affects the structural strength of the surfaces. Correct by 29-
April-2026. 
3.75(c)(2) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
The facility is not adequately maintaining surfaces in housing facilities on a regular basis. The indoor portion of two 
primary enclosures housing a total of 3 rhesus macaques and a java macaque have multiple areas of peeling paint that 
exposes the concrete underneath the paint rendering these areas difficult to effectively clean and sanitize. Peeling paint 
can also be hazardous to the non-human primates if ingested. Additionally, there is an approximately 18 inch x 9 inch 
area of the ceiling in the primary enclosure housing the rhesus macaque and java macaque that is damaged exposing the 
raw wood underneath and creating jagged edges that could result in injury to the animals.  
 
Surfaces in non-human primate housing facilities require regular maintenance to keep them in good repair, to allow them 
to be readily cleaned and sanitized and to protect the animals from injury. All surfaces of non-human primate housing 
facilities must be maintained on a regular basis. Correct by 29-April-2026. 
3.84(a) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The facility is not adequately removing excreta and food waste daily from primary enclosures. The indoor portion of two 
primary enclosures housing a total of 4 brown capuchins and 1 squirrel monkey respectively was observed with excessive 
food waste and excreta on the floor of the enclosures.  
 
Excreta and food waste left in enclosures can lead to increased disease hazards and attract pests that can be harmful to 
the animals. The facility must ensure all excreta and food waste is removed from inside each primary enclosure daily. 
Correct by 29-April-2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.

Additional Inspectors: 
Mark Sanderbeck, ANIMAL CARE INSPECTOR n
